questions.question: Which is the primary benefit of decomposition in CS106A?
options: A) It makes code run faster by default
B) It reduces complexity and improves readability/testability
C) It eliminates all bugs automatically
D) It avoids the need for comments entirely
questions.explanation: Decomposition mainly improves design quality: readability, modularity, and testability. It does not inherently guarantee speed or eliminate all bugs.

questions.question: Which method signature best follows good decomposition for checking evenness?
options: A) void isEven()
B) boolean isEven()
C) boolean isEven(int n)
D) int isEven(int n)
questions.explanation: The method needs input (n) and returns a boolean result, so boolean isEven(int n) is appropriate.

questions.question: A precondition is best described as:
options: A) A guarantee after a method executes
B) A requirement before calling a method
C) The same as a return value
D) The method’s runtime complexity
questions.explanation: Preconditions are requirements on inputs/state that must hold when a method is called.

questions.question: Which code smell suggests poor decomposition?
options: A) Multiple short helper methods with clear names
B) A single 150-line method doing input, processing, and output
C) Using constants for configuration values
D) Documented pre/postconditions
questions.explanation: An overly long, multi-purpose method indicates low cohesion and high complexity.

questions.question: You have methods readData(), computeStats(), and printReport(). What design principle is most improved?
options: A) Coupling increases
B) Cohesion increases
C) Abstraction decreases
D) Runtime complexity decreases automatically
questions.explanation: Each method handles one focused responsibility, increasing cohesion.

questions.question: Which option minimizes coupling between methods?
options: A) Accessing and modifying global variables in helpers
B) Passing only needed values as parameters and returning results
C) Using many public fields across classes
D) Performing I/O inside every helper
questions.explanation: Passing required data and returning results reduces shared state and interdependencies.

questions.question: Given: // requires: arr.length > 0 // ensures: returns max element. Which is best?
options: A) int max(int[] arr)
B) void max(int[] arr)
C) boolean max(int[] arr)
D) String max(int[] arr)
questions.explanation: The method returns the maximum as an int; it needs the array as input.

questions.question: When starting a large assignment, why write method stubs first?
options: A) They ensure optimal performance
B) They allow code to compile and support incremental development/testing
C) They eliminate the need for unit tests
D) They auto-generate method bodies
questions.explanation: Stubs let you compile and integrate structure early, then fill in logic incrementally.

questions.question: Which redesign best increases cohesion?
options: A) Method process() handles input, parse, compute, print
B) Split into readInput(), parse(), compute(), print()
C) Move all code into main()
D) Replace parameters with global variables
questions.explanation: Separating responsibilities into distinct methods increases cohesion and clarity.

questions.question: You have two helpers: normalize(String s) and tokenize(String s). A new feature needs case-insensitive tokenization. What change keeps low coupling?
options: A) Have tokenize() read from System.in directly
B) Store s in a global and let both helpers access it
C) Call normalize() first, then pass its result to tokenize()
D) Merge both into one large method
questions.explanation: Compose helpers by passing outputs to inputs; avoid global state and monolithic methods.
